title: iOS26 SwiftUI for Web Developers
text:
Preface



This is a book aimed at others on a similar journey of specialisation in Swift - from the world of React and web development.






Important



This is *not a legitimate Oreilly publishing, I hope it is one day.


Writing this book as I ask AI to explain concepts to me, and making a shareable resource in the process
The dogs on the cover are Annie and Anubis they are adopted Greyhounds, recommend this breed to any software engineer wanting dogs - they are couch potatoes and will not bother you while you code.
This is dedicated to my wonderful partner Alex; who showed me the world of software engineering and gave me the power to build anything I could dream of. Thankyou for believing in me, and pushing me to learn to code. You were right, it rocks.

title: Introduction
text: If you are autistic, you’ll like the way this is very factually written rather than any fluff or personal stories.

1  Thinking in Swift
text:
1.1 What is Swift?
Welcome to Swift. If you are coming from JavaScript and React like me, this guide will help you translate your web development knowledge into iOS and macOS development.
Swift is Apple’s primary programming language for building iOS and macOS applications.

title: 1  Thinking in Swift
1  Thinking in Swift
text:
1.3 Comparing Swift to JavaScript & React
For developers coming from the web, many concepts in Swift and SwiftUI have direct parallels in JavaScript and React.








Feature
Swift & SwiftUI
JavaScript & React




Language Paradigm
Strongly typed, heavily Protocol-Oriented
Dynamically typed, heavily Functional / Prototype-based


Execution
Compiled to machine code directly (Fast & Safe)
Interpreted / JIT compiled via browser engine


Safety
Errors caught at compile time
Errors caught at runtime (unless using TypeScript)


Core UI Blocks
View structs
React Components (Functions returning JSX)


Syntax Style
Declarative Swift
Declarative JSX


Local State
@State private var count = 0
const [count, setCount] = useState(0)


Passed State (Props)
let title: String or @Binding
Component props


Global State
@EnvironmentObject or @Observable
Context API, Redux, Zustand

title: 1  Thinking in Swift
text:
1.3 Two Core Concepts of Swift
When transitioning from JavaScript to Swift, these are the two most important differences to understand:

1. Swift is Object and Protocol-Oriented






ImportantEverything in Swift is an object.



Fact: Basic data types like integers, strings, and booleans are fully functioning objects with their own properties and methods, unlike the primitive types found in JavaScript.
let age = 30
// 'age' is an Int object. We can call methods directly on it:
let isMultiple = age.isMultiple(of: 5) // returns true


Why is Swift designed this way? - Interoperability: The original Apple frameworks (Cocoa Touch) were built strictly on Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) paradigms. Swift needed to seamlessly interoperate with this massive existing ecosystem. - Protocol-Oriented Programming: Swift expands on traditional OOP. Instead of focusing only on what objects are (via inheritance), Swift places a heavy emphasis on what objects can do using Protocols (similar to Interfaces in TypeScript).


2. Swift is Strongly and Statically Typed






ImportantEverything in Swift has a specific type.



Fact: The compiler enforces these types before the app can run.
var name = "Charlotte" // The compiler infers this is a String

// The compiler immediately flags this as an error before the app ever runs:
// name = 42 


How this differs from JavaScript: - JavaScript: You can dynamically change a variable from a number to a string. Type errors are only caught at runtime (when the user is using the app), which can cause crashes. - Swift: Type errors are caught at compile-time (while you are writing the code). You cannot compile or run the app until type errors are fixed. This drastically reduces bugs in production. - Type Inference: Swift’s compiler automatically figures out the type of a variable based on its initial value. This gives you the safety of strict types without the need to manually declare them every time.


3. The Compile Cycle: What happens when you hit build?
Fact: Swift compiles directly down to native machine code (binary). Unlike React, which is shipped as raw text (JavaScript) to be read and interpreted by a user’s web browser at runtime, an iOS app is fully “pre-baked” before it ever leaves your computer.
The Build Process (Step-by-step):

You write .swift code (Human readable logic).
LLVM Compiler engages. It strictly checks for type-errors, missing variables, or syntactic anomalies. If it finds even one error, the build forcibly halts.
Translation into Machine Code. If your code passes all checks, LLVM translates your logic directly into highly optimized 1s and 0s (ARM Machine Code) specifically tailored for an iPhone’s processor.
Packaging into an .ipa. This compiled binary, alongside your images and configuration files, is securely zipped into an .ipa (iOS App Store Package) file.

What actually ships to the App Store?

You do not ship your raw source code.
You ship the finalized, fully compiled .ipa binary package.
Why this matters for your users: Because the app arrives on their physical phone already translated into raw machine code, it opens and runs blazingly fast compared to a web browser downloading and parsing a heavy JavaScript bundle.

title: 1  Thinking in Swift
text:
Key facts about Swift:

Introduced: 2014 by Apple.
Purpose: To replace Objective-C with a safer, faster, and more modern language.
Creator: Chris Lattner. He previously built LLVM, which is the compiler infrastructure that now powers Swift.

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
2.1 The Folder Structure
Welcome to the anatomy of an iOS application. In this chapter, we break down exactly how a Swift app functions at a high level.
Every iOS project natively uses Xcode as the development environment. The folder structure relies on specific files existing in order for the application to compile correctly.
High-Level Xcode Structure:
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
App.swift — The root entry point of the app.
ContentView.swift — The first screen the user actually sees.
Assets.xcassets — Where you store images, custom colors, and app icons.
Info.plist — Configuration file containing hardware permissions and app rules.







NoteWhat does this look like in React?





React projects are more flexible, but generally map similarly:

index.js (Root Entry)
App.js (Main Router/Screen)
public/ or src/assets/ (Images/Icons)
package.json (Configuration)
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App
text:
2.4 Answering Core Architectural Questions
It’s common to wonder how the environment handles processing work under the hood. Here are the clear, factual answers mapped from the code above.

1. Is this single-threaded?
Fact: UI rendering in Swift is purely single-threaded.

All interface rendering runs on what Apple calls the Main Thread.
In the code block above, when you tap the button, that line of code immediately executes on the Main Thread to guarantee a buttery-smooth 60 to 120 frames per second response.
Rule: If you fetch data from the internet (which is slow), you must explicitly push that task to a “Background Thread” to avoid freezing the screen.



2. When does the render happen?
Fact: Rendering is reactive, triggered purely by a change in State.

When the button is tapped and userName changes from “Charlotte” to “Swift Developer”, SwiftUI detects the change in @State and immediately invalidates the current body.
SwiftUI then re-computes the physical screen to match the updated state. This is highly optimized and functionally matching how React triggers a re-render when you call setState().



3. Where is the data stored?
Fact: Local state data is stored specifically inside the Struct instance using Property Wrappers.

Structs are highly efficient, temporary data structures.
The data (userName) only exists for as long as this specific screen is visually alive on the device.
If you have global data (state you want to share across multiple screens), you store it in classes called ObservableObjects, which act very much like React Context or an overarching Redux Store.
crumbs: 2  Anatomy of a Basic App

title: 3  Swift Syntax & Lexical Rules
text:
3.1 The Rules of Syntax
Welcome to the lexical breakdown of Swift. When transitioning from JavaScript, you will notice Swift is structurally cleaner, strictly enforced by the compiler, and relies heavily on minimizing visual clutter.
Here is a fast, factual rundown of the core lexical rules in Swift.

title: 3  Swift Syntax & Lexical Rules
4  Layout Building Blocks (Stacks)
text:
4.1 The <div> Equivalent
Welcome to SwiftUI layouts. In HTML and React, developers reach for native <div> elements and CSS Flexbox to physically position items on the screen. In Swift, you explicitly use Stacks, which are structurally safer and highly optimized pre-configured wrappers.
Fact: Stacks act conceptually identically to div tags, but they come with pre-baked flexbox styles specifically tailored for mobile and desktop screens. - In React, a div is structurally dumb. You must explicitly apply display: flex; flex-direction: column just to format items sequentially. - In Swift, Stacks are inherently intelligent and are implicitly pre-configured to handle flexible layout distribution and alignment.

title: 4  Layout Building Blocks (Stacks)
4  Layout Building Blocks (Stacks)
8  @ViewBuilder
11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
11.1 Quick-Fire Questions & Answers
Welcome to the interview prep zone. After absorbing structural knowledge, here is a literal, rapid-fire breakdown of the questions you are highly likely to encounter when interviewing for an iOS role—especially if they know you are transitioning from web development.
No fluff. Just the raw architectural facts wrapped in clear answers, heavily visualizing what you actually say out loud.
crumbs: 11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture

title: 11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
1. “What is the difference between @State and @Binding?”
The Fact: @State physically owns the data locally. @Binding physically creates a reference to data owned by someone else.

Answer Structure: “In SwiftUI, @State is used to create and explicitly own local data inside a specific view. When you use @Binding inside a child view, you are simply passing down direct, two-way access to that parent’s @State variable without duplicating the physical data.”
React Parallel: “It is functionally identical to passing down a useState’s setter function (setCount) via props to a child component, allowing the child to mutate the parent’s data.”



2. “Why use a Struct instead of a Class for SwiftUI views?”
The Fact: Structs are “Value Types” and strictly immutable. Classes are “Reference Types” and require complex memory tracking.

Answer Structure: “SwiftUI Views are explicitly built using Structs because they are lightweight. This means every time the layout state changes, the system can instantly destroy and seamlessly recreate the view virtually cost-free. If we used Classes, tracking physical state differences would be slow because Classes live on the explicit memory heap and require complex reference tracking.”



3. “Explain the MVVM Architecture.”
The Fact: MVVM cleanly isolates visual UI logic from raw data execution.

Answer Structure: “MVVM strictly splits logic into three boxes.

Model: The raw physical data shape (like a JSON struct).
View: The purely visual SwiftUI component you see on screen.
ViewModel: The operational brain. This is an ObservableObject class that securely fetches the data, processes it, and cleanly hands it to the View.”

Visual Rule: The View should fundamentally never make a direct API call itself. It explicitly explicitly asks the ViewModel to execute the work.



4. “Is Swift Pass-by-Value or Pass-by-Reference?”
The Fact: Swift execution fundamentally depends on exactly what physical structure you are sending out.

Answer Structure: “It depends strictly on the Type. Structs and Enums are passed by value (they are literally physically duplicated when passed). Classes are explicitly passed by reference (a memory pointer to the exact identical location is shared).”



5. “What is Protocol-Oriented Programming (POP)?”
The Fact: POP forcibly requires developers to standardize what objects can do rather than what objects inherit from.

Answer Structure: “Instead of creating massive fragile, inherited Class trees (like Vehicle -> Car -> ElectricCar), Swift uses Protocols. A Protocol acts identically to an Interface in TypeScript. You give a Struct a protocol (like Drivable), natively guaranteeing it possesses specific functions, completely bypassing the massive structural nightmares typical iteration inheritance creates.”



6. “How does SwiftUI handle the Main Thread?”
The Fact: Any code that updates the user interface visually absolutely must occur on the explicit Main Thread.

Answer Structure: “UI updates always run exclusively on the Main Thread to guarantee an unbreakable 60fps frame rate. If I pull data from the internet, I explicitly forceful move that heavy processing to a Background Thread. Once the data completely arrives, I strategically inject it back onto the Main Thread using @MainActor or DispatchQueue.main.async so the screen visually updates without stuttering.”
crumbs: 11  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture

Fact: Stacks act conceptually identically to div tags, but they come with pre-baked flexbox styles specifically tailored for mobile and desktop screens. - In React, a div is structurally dumb. You must explicitly apply display: flex; flex-direction: column just to format items sequentially. - In Swift, Stacks are inherently intelligent and are implicitly pre-configured to handle flexible layout distribution and alignment.

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
13.1 What exactly is it?
Welcome to the native database system of iOS. As your app grows, you will inevitably need to save data permanently so it safely survives when the user forcibly closes the app. You accomplish this using a specialized file visually represented as .xcdatamodeld, which powers Apple’s highly optimized Core Data (or the newer SwiftData) framework.
Fact: The .xcdatamodeld file is the blueprint for a fully functioning, high-performance local database entirely built into the iOS system itself.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
Production Grade: It is explicitly used for Production apps. This is exactly how massive scale apps (like Twitter, Spotify, and Apple Notes) securely store gigabytes of user data permanently on your physical phone.
Standalone: It completely replaces the structural need to rely on linking an external local database dependency.


How does it conceptually map to Web Storage?






NoteIs it like localStorage in the browser?





No. localStorage is extremely fragile. It is strictly limited to 5MB, mechanically converts everything down into a raw String, and gets forcefully wiped if the user clears their cache mapping.
To achieve exact localStorage-like behavior in iOS (for storing tiny items like specifically setting a “Dark Mode” toggle or caching a user token), Swift uses a feature called User Defaults. We never use .xcdatamodeld for tiny settings.









NoteIs it like SQLite or IndexedDB?





Yes. Under the explicit hood, .xcdatamodeld literally commands an optimized SQLite relational database file physically hidden in the phone’s sandboxed file system. It structurally handles complex relational mapping constraints, massive tables manipulating millions of rows, offline storage caching buffers, and instant synchronization.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
13.2 How .xcdatamodeld Works Visually
Fact: Unlike traditional Web databases where you forcefully write out raw SQL string queries (like SELECT * FROM users), Xcode deliberately provides a purely graphical visual interface.
When you click on the .xcdatamodeld file in Xcode:

You see a visual flowchart map.
You graphically add an “Entity” (A Database Table). You might physically name it User.
You graphically add “Attributes” (Table Columns). You explicitly attach an age as a strict Integer, and name as a strict String.

The Mechanical Magic: Swift physically analyzes your visual blueprint map inside Xcode and automatically, silently generates the exact matching object-oriented Swift Classes necessary to execute logic against it in the compiler background.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 13  Persistent Storage (.xcdatamodeld)
text:
13.4 Core Architectural Rules

Production Permanence: This is a highly hardened, explicitly engineered, massive-scale production-ready storage architecture—not a simple development mock.
Types are Absolute: If you graphically define age as an Integer in the .xcdatamodeld map, the database codebase will explicitly throw a compiler error if you mistakenly attempt to save the string "twenty".
Cloud Connected: Apple natively includes a literal toggle switch inside the Xcode configuration that dynamically, seamlessly syncs this local SQLite database to iCloud—explicitly duplicating state cross-platform (Watch, Mac, and iPhone) with zero lines of manual synchronization code.
When is another database needed? Fact: .xcdatamodeld is strictly “Local-First”. If your app demands real-time multi-user visibility (like a shared chat room, global high scores, or feeding data to external web users), you explicitly need a remote database (like Supabase, Firebase, or a custom backend) to act as the global server. Coré Data is then typically used as an offline cache to physically hold what it downloads from that external server.
Can sensitive data live here? Fact: Absolutely not. While .xcdatamodeld is sandboxed within the app, it is not heavily encrypted against a deliberately compromised device. You must never store raw user passwords, financial tokens, or API keys here.

Rule: High-security sensitive strings are exclusively securely saved into the Keychain, an entirely separate, specialized hardware-encrypted vault physically built into Apple devices.
crumbs: Part III: Data Flow and Architecture

title: 15  Model View ViewModel
text:
This is based on this wonderful repository by (kudoleh?) iOS Clean Architecture MVVM. Let’s systematically break down Clean Architecture and MVVM on iOS, layer by layer. The goal is complete separation of concerns.





flowchart TD
    classDef default fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000;
    subgraph Presentation[Presentation Layer]
        UI[Views / UI]
        VM[ViewModels]
    end
    style Presentation fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph Domain[Domain Layer]
        UC[Use Cases]
        E[Entities]
        RI[Repository Interfaces]
    end
    style Domain fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph Data[Data Layer]
        RI_Impl[Repository Implementations]
        API[API / Network]
        DB[Local DB / CoreData]
    end
    style Data fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    
    UI <-->|Observes/Actions| VM
    VM -->|Executes| UC
    UC -->|Uses| E
    UC -->|Requests from| RI
    RI_Impl -.->|Implements| RI
    RI_Impl --> API
    RI_Impl --> DB







Dependency Direction
The most important rule in Clean Architecture: Inner layers do not depend on outer layers.
Presentation Layer -> Domain Layer <- Data Layer

Presentation Layer depends on the Domain Layer.
Data Layer depends on the Domain Layer.
Domain Layer depends on nothing outside of itself. It is the isolated core containing your business rules.




1. Domain Layer
The Domain Layer is the conceptual heart of your application. It contains the core business rules and logic. It must not import any UI frameworks (like UIKit or SwiftUI) or data frameworks (like CoreData or Alamofire).

Entities: Pure Swift structs or classes representing the raw data models of your business (e.g., Movie, User).
Use Cases (Interactors): Classes that define specific application actions (e.g., SearchMoviesUseCase, FetchRecentQueriesUseCase). They combine data from entities and repositories.
Repository Interfaces (Protocols): Protocols that define how data should be fetched or saved. By defining these here, the Domain Layer dictates what data it needs without caring how it is retrieved (Dependency Inversion).



2. Presentation Layer
The Presentation Layer is responsible for what the user sees and interacts with. It uses the MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) pattern to separate UI rendering from UI logic.

Views (UI): SwiftUI Views or UIViewControllers. They display data and capture user input. They have no business logic.
ViewModels: They handle presentation logic. They take input from the View, execute Domain Use Cases, and expose observable state back to the View. They should be completely independent of UI frameworks, making them easily testable and reusable across UIKit and SwiftUI replacements.
Flow Coordinators: Usually placed here to manage navigation between views, reducing the burden on individual views and view models.



3. Data Layer
The Data Layer implements the interfaces defined by the Domain Layer. It handles data retrieval, storage, and mapping.

Repository Implementations: Conforms to the Domain Layer’s repository interfaces. This is where you decide where the data actually comes from (e.g., fetching from an API first, then falling back to a cached local database).
Data Transfer Objects (DTOs): Intermediate models used for parsing JSON (e.g., Decodable) or mapping to local databases (NSManagedObject). They are strictly part of this layer and are mapped to pure Domain Entities before returning to the Domain Layer.



4. Infrastructure Layer (Network)
Often considered a part of (or a supporting boundary for) the Data Layer.

Network Wrapper: A wrapper around your networking engine (like Alamofire or URLSession). It manages endpoints, base URLs, and raw data mapping. By isolating networking here, you can easily swap out networking libraries without rewriting your data repositories.



The Data Flow Lifecycle

View receives a user interaction (e.g., tapping a search button).
View calls a method on the ViewModel.
ViewModel executes a Use Case from the Domain Layer.
Use Case requests data via a Repository Interface.
Repository Implementation (Data Layer) fetches data from a remote API or local database (Infrastructure Layer).
Data Layer maps the raw response (DTO) into a clean Entity and returns it.
Use Case applies business rules and returns the result to the ViewModel.
ViewModel updates its observable state.
View observes the state change and automatically re-renders the UI.

title: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
16.1 Quick-Fire Questions & Answers
Welcome to the interview prep zone. After absorbing structural knowledge, here is a literal, rapid-fire breakdown of the questions you are highly likely to encounter when interviewing for an iOS role—especially if they know you are transitioning from web development.
No fluff. Just the raw architectural facts wrapped in clear answers, heavily visualizing what you actually say out loud.
crumbs: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture

title: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture
text:
1. “What is the difference between @State and @Binding?”
The Fact: @State physically owns the data locally. @Binding physically creates a reference to data owned by someone else.

Is the binding reactive? Yes. Because @Binding is a direct two-way structural tunnel to the parent’s @State, any changes made by the child instantly mutate the parent’s data, natively triggering a reactive update.
What happens to the view? The moment the underlying @State mutates (whether altered by the parent or flipped via the child’s @Binding), SwiftUI instantly invalidates both views and seamlessly re-renders them to reflect the new data visually.
Explicit React Equivalents:

@State = const [count, setCount] = useState().
@Binding = Passing the setCount mutating function down as a physical prop: <Child increment={setCount} />.

Answer Structure: “In SwiftUI, @State is used to firmly create and own local data inside a specific view. When you use @Binding inside a child view, you are simply passing down direct, two-way reactive access to that parent’s @State variable without ever duplicating the physical memory.”



2. “Why use a Struct instead of a Class for SwiftUI views?”
The Fact: Structs are “Value Types” and strictly immutable. Classes are “Reference Types” and require complex memory tracking.

Answer Structure: “SwiftUI Views are explicitly built using Structs because they are lightweight. This means every time the layout state changes, the system can instantly destroy and seamlessly recreate the view virtually cost-free. If we used Classes, tracking physical state differences would be slow because Classes live on the explicit memory heap and require complex reference tracking.”



3. “Explain the MVVM Architecture.”
The Fact: MVVM cleanly isolates visual UI logic from raw data execution.

Answer Structure: “MVVM strictly splits logic into three boxes.

Model: The raw physical data shape (like a JSON struct).
View: The purely visual SwiftUI component you see on screen.
ViewModel: The operational brain. This is an ObservableObject class that securely fetches the data, processes it, and cleanly hands it to the View.”

Visual Rule: The View should fundamentally never make a direct API call itself. It explicitly explicitly asks the ViewModel to execute the work.



4. “Is Swift Pass-by-Value or Pass-by-Reference?”
The Fact: Swift execution fundamentally depends on exactly what physical structure you are sending out.

Answer Structure: “It depends strictly on the Type. Structs and Enums are passed by value (they are literally physically duplicated when passed). Classes are explicitly passed by reference (a memory pointer to the exact identical location is shared).”



5. “What is Protocol-Oriented Programming (POP)?”
The Fact: POP forcibly requires developers to standardize what objects can do rather than what objects inherit from.

Answer Structure: “Instead of creating massive fragile, inherited Class trees (like Vehicle -> Car -> ElectricCar), Swift uses Protocols. A Protocol acts identically to an Interface in TypeScript. You give a Struct a protocol (like Drivable), natively guaranteeing it possesses specific functions, completely bypassing the massive structural nightmares typical iteration inheritance creates.”



6. “How does SwiftUI handle the Main Thread?”
The Fact: Any code that updates the user interface visually absolutely must occur on the explicit Main Thread.

Answer Structure: “UI updates always run exclusively on the Main Thread to guarantee an unbreakable 60fps frame rate. If I pull data from the internet, I explicitly forceful move that heavy processing to a Background Thread. Once the data completely arrives, I strategically inject it back onto the Main Thread using @MainActor or DispatchQueue.main.async so the screen visually updates without stuttering.”
crumbs: 16  Interview Prep: Swift & iOS Architecture

title: 11  Don’t Panic
text:
Xcode has so many ways it can make you feel so anxious, frustrated, and upset. Let’s go through them so you know what is a usual issue.

Ghost Files - Files that are hidden
Sometimes you can see files in your IDE (VScode / Antigravity) that do not exist in the xcode same exact project.
Right click on a folder in Xcode and click "Add Files" to see non greyed out files that are in the same folder as your project. make sure that the files are added to the project.
Even though the file is in the correct folder, Xcode won’t acknowledge it until you manually re-link it inside the IDE via File > Add Files To "YourProject".
— ## References
For an official deeper dive into exactly how Xcode manages literal file maps versus physical folder mappings: - Managing Files and Folders in Your Xcode Project (Apple Inc. 2026)




Apple Inc. 2026. “Managing Files and Folders in Your Xcode Project.” 2026. https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode/managing-files-and-folders-in-your-xcode-project.

title: 15  Model View ViewModel (MVVM)
text:
Dependency Direction
This is based on this wonderful repository by (kudoleh?) iOS Clean Architecture MVVM. Let’s systematically break down Clean Architecture and MVVM on iOS, layer by layer. The goal is complete separation of concerns.
The most important rule in Clean Architecture: Inner layers do not depend on outer layers.
Presentation Layer -> Domain Layer <- Data Layer

title: 15  Model View ViewModel (MVVM)
text:
15.1 Case Study: “The Movies App”
To make this concrete, let’s look at the “Movies App” example from the reference repository. The goal of the app is simple: it allows a user to search for movies, see a list of results, and view details about a selected movie. It also caches recent searches so they work offline.
Let’s map our abstract layer concepts directly to the actual code structures in this app.





flowchart TD
    classDef default fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000;
    
    subgraph Presentation[Presentation Layer]
        UI[MoviesListView<br>MoviesListViewController]
        VM[MoviesListViewModel]
        Coord[MoviesSearchFlowCoordinator]
    end
    style Presentation fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph Domain[Domain Layer]
        UC[SearchMoviesUseCase]
        E[Movie<br>MovieQuery]
        RI[MoviesRepository Protocol]
    end
    style Domain fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph Data[Data Layer]
        RI_Impl[DefaultMoviesRepository]
        API[MoviesResponseDTO]
        DB[MoviesResponseEntity<br>CoreData]
    end
    style Data fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    
    UI <-->|Observes/Actions| VM
    Coord -.->|Listens for Routing| VM
    VM -->|Executes| UC
    UC -->|Uses| E
    UC -->|Requests from| RI
    RI_Impl -.->|Implements| RI
    RI_Impl --> API
    RI_Impl --> DB







1. Domain Layer in Action
The Domain Layer sets the rules. Here we define Movie as a simple struct. We define SearchMoviesUseCase, which expects a search query and returns a list of Movies. Crucially, the Use Case doesn’t know how to fetch movies. It just talks to a protocol: MoviesRepository. The Domain says: “I need a repository that can fulfill this protocol.”


2. Presentation Layer in Action
The Presentation Layer drives the UI. We have MoviesListView which handles the visual layout (e.g., a search bar and list). This view observes MoviesListViewModel. When the user types “Inception”, the View tells the ViewModel. The ViewModel calls SearchMoviesUseCase.start(query: "Inception"). When the Use Case returns an array of Movie structs, the ViewModel updates its observable state, and the View automatically updates the screen. Meanwhile, MoviesSearchFlowCoordinator observes the ViewModel to know when it should push the Movie Detail screen onto the navigation stack.


3. Data Layer in Action
The Data Layer does the dirty work. DefaultMoviesRepository implements the MoviesRepository protocol from the Domain Layer. When asked for movies, it first checks its persistent storage (like CoreData, using MoviesResponseEntity). If it doesn’t have the data, it asks the API. It receives JSON, decodes it using MoviesResponseDTO (Data Transfer Object), maps it cleanly into our pure Swift Movie struct, and passes it back up to the Domain Layer. Because of this separation, if we decided to swap CoreData for Realm, the UI and Domain wouldn’t need to change a single line of code!



What is a DIContainer?
In Clean Architecture, because inner layers (like the Domain) cannot know about outer layers (like Network or Data Repositories), we rely heavily on Dependency Inversion. The Domain simply says: “I need something that conforms to MoviesRepository.” But who actually creates the DefaultMoviesRepository and hands it to the Domain?
This is the job of the Dependency Injection Container (DIContainer).
A DIContainer is a central factory object that wires your entire app together. It usually lives at the very top level of your app (often in an AppDIContainer or SceneDIContainer). Its responsibilities are to:

Hold Shared Resources: Keep track of long-lived dependencies (like a single APIClient instance or a shared CoreDataStorage).
Build the Dependency Graph: When a View needs a ViewModel, the DIContainer builds the ViewModel. To do that, it builds the required Use Case. To do that, it builds the required Repository.

By keeping all setup and instantiation logic inside a DIContainer, your Views and ViewModels don’t need to know how to build their own dependencies. This keeps your layers loosely coupled and makes your code incredibly easy to test (because you can simply inject a “Mock” repository instead of the real one during a test).

title: 16  Domain Driven Design (DDD)
text:
If MVVM tells you how to separate your UI from your logic, Domain Driven Design (DDD) tells you how to structure the logic itself.
DDD is an approach to building software centered entirely around the business domain. The primary goal is that your code must speak the exact same language as your business experts. No artificial developer translation layers.
Let’s look at the core building blocks of DDD in an iOS context.





flowchart TD
    classDef default fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000;
    
    subgraph BoundedContextA [Bounded Context: Checkout]
        AggA[Aggregate: Order]
        EntA[Entity: OrderItem]
        VOA[Value Object: Currency]
        
        AggA --> EntA
        EntA --> VOA
    end
    style BoundedContextA fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph BoundedContextB [Bounded Context: Profile]
        AggB[Aggregate: Customer]
        EntB[Entity: CustomerRecord]
        VOB[Value Object: MailingAddress]
        
        AggB --> EntB
        EntB --> VOB
    end
    style BoundedContextB fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    
    BoundedContextA -.->|Strictly Isolated| BoundedContextB







The 4 Core Concepts

1. Ubiquitous Language
Stop using developer jargon (UserDataManager, NetworkHandler) when talking to subject matter experts. Use the exact vocabulary they use. If the business calls it a “Customer Profile”, your struct must be named CustomerProfile. If they call the checkout process “Fulfillment”, your Swift module must be named Fulfillment.


2. Bounded Contexts
Don’t build one massive, tangled app-wide data model. Treat completely separate features of your app as distinct universes (Bounded Contexts). A User in the Profile context needs fields like bio and avatar. But a User in the Checkout context only needs a paymentToken and shippingAddress. Instead of creating a giant User struct packed with unused optional fields, create two separate distinct models within their own Bounded Context folders.


3. Entities vs Value Objects
When designing your raw data structures, categorize them strictly into two buckets:

Entities: Defined by their Identity. They change over time, but their ID remains the same.

Example: A Customer(id: 123, name: "Alice"). If Alice changes her name to Bob, it’s still the exact same customer because the ID is 123.
iOS execution: Often a class or a struct explicitly conforming to Identifiable.

Value Objects: Defined entirely by their Attributes. They have no ID. They are perfectly interchangeable and strictly immutable.

Example: An Address(zip: "90210"). If the zip code changes, it is not the same address anymore. It is a completely new Address.
iOS execution: Pure, immutable let only struct.




4. Aggregates
An Aggregate is a cluster of Entities and Value Objects treated as a single unit or boundary. For example, an Order (Aggregate Root) contains OrderItems (Entities) and a ShippingAddress (Value Object). You should never interact with an OrderItem directly from outside the aggregate. You must go through the Order. If you want to modify an item, you tell the aggregate root: order.updateItem(...).




How DDD Shapes Your Folders
The most immediate practical impact DDD has on your Xcode project is folder structure. Instead of grouping files by their “Type” (like MVC does), you group them by their Feature or “Bounded Context”.
The Wrong Way (Grouped by File Type)
Models/
  - Product.swift
  - User.swift
Views/
  - ProductList.swift
  - UserProfile.swift
The DDD Way (Grouped by Feature / Bounded Context)
Catalog/ (Bounded Context)
  - Domain/
    - Product.swift
  - Presentation/
    - ProductList.swift

Account/ (Bounded Context)
  - Domain/
    - User.swift
  - Presentation/
    - UserProfile.swift
By structuring your codebase by feature, you natively isolate your domains. You could easily drag and drop the entire Catalog folder into a completely different app prototype, because it doesn’t hopelessly tangle itself with the Account logic!

title: 17  Modern Clean Architecture for mobile applications
text:
If we combine the deep decoupling of Clean Architecture with the modularization strategies of Domain-Driven Design (DDD), we arrive at a highly scalable setup often called Modern Clean Architecture.
The goal here isn’t just separation for the sake of it. The primary motivation is to keep the design of each layer incredibly easy to understand, trivially maintainable, and mathematically isolated to drastically protect build times.





flowchart TD
    classDef default fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000;
    
    subgraph Composition [Root]
        App[Application Layer]
    end
    style Composition fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph UI [User Interfaces]
        Features[Features Layer<br>MVVM / TCA]
    end
    style UI fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph Business [Core]
        Domain[Domain Layer<br>Entities & Use Cases]
    end
    style Business fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    subgraph Infra [Plugins & Hardware]
        Platform[Platform Layer<br>API / DB Impls]
        Core[Core Layer<br>HTTP / Tools]
    end
    style Infra fill:transparent,stroke:#999,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    App -->|Wires| Features
    App -->|Instantiates| Platform
    App -.->|Injects dynamically| Domain
    
    Features -->|Renders| Domain
    Platform -->|Implements protocols| Domain
    Platform -->|Uses| Core







The 5 Architectural Layers

1. Domain Layer
This encapsulates the absolute core business logic. It contains Entities, Value Objects, Aggregates, and Use Case protocols. It isolates the business logic so that application-specific concerns (like UI or networking) absolutely cannot pollute the math. It depends on nothing else.


2. Features Layer
This implements the UI requirements and builds individual screens. Because everything else is decoupled, your choice of UI pattern becomes far less critical here! You can use MVVM for your Profile feature and switch to TCA (The Composable Architecture) for complex Checkout flows without issue. * Navigation: Managed via the Coordinator pattern (essentially just the Delegate pattern). The ViewModel blindly delegates navigation side effects out to a Coordinator protocol (coordinator.showDetail(for: movie)).


3. Application Layer
The absolute top-level of your app. This layer is responsible for creating the main structural navigation hierarchy, instantiating concrete implementations of UseCases from the Platform layer, and injecting them into the Features layer at runtime. Because it constructs the entire interface, it uniquely holds the total context required to manage the app-wide navigation stack.


4. Platform Layer (The Plugin Model)
This layer utilizes the raw toolkits built in the Core layer to actually fulfill the protocols defined in the Domain layer. For example, a MoviesAPI module uses a generic HTTPClient (from Core) to fulfill the MoviesGateway protocol (from Domain). This forces a strict plugin design: if you suddenly need to change your networking from REST to GraphQL, you simply swap the plugin out in the Platform layer. The Features UI and Domain math remain completely untouched.


5. Core Layer
The very bottom foundational infrastructure. This includes your primitive HTTP networking wrappers, SwiftData extensions, caching mechanisms, and generic utilities utilized by the Platform layer to build its concrete plugins.




The Ultimate Payout: Developer Productivity
Why go through all this trouble to aggressively enforce dependency rules? Xcode Build times.
In this architecture, a Feature module (like MoviesUI) only depends on the Domain module (pure Swift protocols and structs) and SwiftUI. It does not depend on the Platform, Application, or Core layers.
This means when you modify the MoviesUI code, Xcode only has to compile that specific UI Feature and the Domain layer. Your day-to-day SwiftUI work can rely on instant Xcode Previews and feature-specific mini-apps without ever suffering the heavy compilation times of the networking, CoreData, or global application boot sequence!
crumbs: 17  Modern Clean Architecture for mobile applications
